trajectory_summary: The call opens neutral and briefly reaches a conditionally open state when Kevin volunteers his bill data and explicitly says he is willing to listen. Meg does not pick up either signal. She leads with a shade concern sourced from Google Maps, accepts Kevin's tree-removal refusal as a fatal constraint, and self-DQs the lead before completing verify, before routing to a closer, and before deploying a single curiosity hook. The call ends with a vague 'give me a call if things change' exit — no locked callback, no re-engagement structure. Energy leaked from the moment Meg framed shade as the opening concern.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is a self-DQ by the rep. This is premature. Kevin said 'I'm more willing to listen' at 01:48 — a direct invitation — and his bill data ($76–$109/month) was volunteered, not extracted. The rep closed the lead on a shade impression and a tree-removal preference, neither of which is a hard DQ in the schema. The correct disposition was to route to the closer's shading analysis tools, not to remove Kevin from the list. This call should be reclassified from DQ to missed-sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Setter-scope DQ discipline — shade is a closer's call, not a setter's
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So, yeah, like you said, you're not open to removing the trees at all, so solar won't work for you. I can just remove you from my list.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Meg self-DQ'd a lead that had explicitly said 'I'm more willing to listen' 27 seconds earlier. The shade concern was real but not a schema hard DQ — it is precisely the kind of condition the closer's shading analysis tools exist to evaluate. This specific DQ was premature given Kevin's residual openness. The manager should walk Meg through the hard DQ list (dq_rules) and establish a clear rule: if the condition is not on that list, the setter's job is to bridge to the closer, not to adjudicate. The universal escape hatch from coaching_philosophy.setter_scope_discipline is the drill vehicle here.

key_moments.quote: Right. So that's the one that I'm concerned about. It's definitely putting quite a bit of shade. Are you I mean, my average
key_moments.stewart_read: Kevin begins to volunteer his bill data mid-sentence — 'my average' — and Meg talks over him to press the shade concern. This is the earliest evidence of reactive posture: Meg is following her own problem-framing track rather than listening for the customer's signal. The bill anchor, which the schema identifies as the emotional center of the pitch, was being handed to Meg for free and she interrupted it.

By 00:37 Meg has confirmed address and home type. She then moves directly to the shade concern at 00:49. The schema requires 6-of-8 verify items; Meg collects 2 (address/homeowner confirmed, home type). Title/co-owners, utility company, roof type/age, prior designs, interest_in_solar, and residence length are never collected. The call ends without a single additional verify item.

key_moments.quote: well, the highest bill I've ever had was a $109. And my average bill, I'd say, over a course of twelve months is around 76 to $81.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kevin volunteers precise bill data — $76–$81 average, $109 peak — unprompted. This is the bill anchor the schema says must be captured AND flipped. Meg's response at 02:02 acknowledges the low bill ('your bill isn't extremely high') but does not flip it. She uses it as a secondary DQ rationale rather than as an emotional anchor. The flip was available: the bill Kevin is paying is the bill he will keep paying forever if solar does not work — the question is whether the shade analysis confirms that.

key_moments.quote: Breakeven over twenty one years. It used to be a thirty six year payback, so I don't know.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kevin has done prior solar research and is citing specific payback figures (21-year breakeven, previously 36-year). This is a customer who has engaged with solar economics before — he is not uninformed, he is unconvinced. Meg does not address the payback math at any point. The schema calls for 3+ angles per objection; Meg deploys zero. The knowledge gap here is not Kevin's — it is Meg's failure to route a math-literate objection to the closer's design tools rather than accepting it as a DQ.
